title: Oetosis piloselloides
intro:
I couldn't find any information on the plant Oetosis piloselloides. However, based on the name, it seems to be a misspelling or variation of Pilosella piloselloides, also known as Mouse-ear Hawkweed or Pilosella officinarum. 

Here's a paragraph about Pilosella piloselloides:

Pilosella piloselloides, also known as Mouse-ear Hawkweed, is a perennial herbaceous plant that belongs to the dandelion family (Asteraceae). It is traditionally used for its medicinal properties, including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant effects, which can help to alleviate digestive issues, wounds, and skin problems. The bioactive constituents of Pilosella piloselloides include flavonoids, such as quercetin and kaempferol, and sesquiterpene lactones, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, Pilosella piloselloides is often used to make teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to treat various health conditions, including digestive issues, skin problems, and wounds.
taxonomy: Oetosis piloselloides belongs to the family Asteraceae, which is commonly known as the sunflower or daisy family. It is a species of flowering plant in the genus Oetosis. Within the Asteraceae family, Oetosis piloselloides is classified as part of the subfamily Asteroideae. This plant is native to Europe, North Africa, and Western Asia. The classification of Oetosis piloselloides reflects its evolutionary relationships with other plants within the Asteraceae family.
uses: Oetosis piloselloides, also known as Mouse-ear hawk's-beard, is a plant species that has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. Its leaves and stems are rich in antioxidants and have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help soothe skin irritations and reduce swelling. In folk medicine, Oetosis piloselloides has been used to treat digestive issues, such as bloating and indigestion, due to its carminative properties. The plant's leaves have also been used to make a tea that can help alleviate respiratory problems, such as bronchitis and asthma. Further research is needed to confirm the efficacy of Oetosis piloselloides in treating these conditions, but it has shown promise as a natural remedy.

parts: Oetosis piloselloides, also known as false hawk's beard or mouse ears, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes.
